name: holy basil
mechanism: Proposed mechanisms involve phytochemicals such as eugenol and triterpenes with potential anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and neuroendocrine stress-modulating effects; robust dose-response confirmation in humans is limited.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference: Used in ritual

dreaming or folk medicine. Used in ritual
contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless supervised by a qualified clinician.
Do not use with known allergy to this plant or related species.
safetyNotes: Generally tolerated in short-term studies, but long-term safety data and standardized-product safety are limited. Use caution with glucose-lowering or anticoagulant therapies until interaction risk is better characterized.

description: Holy basil is an aromatic Lamiaceae herb used in South Asian traditional medicine and as tea/extract supplements. Commercial products vary in chemotype and standardization, which limits cross-trial comparability.
dosage: No single evidence-based universal dose; studies use varied extract strengths and durations. Prefer standardized products and conservative titration.

interactions: Possible additive glucose-lowering effects with antidiabetic medications.
Potential pharmacodynamic interaction with anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy (limited evidence; monitor clinically).
preparation: Used as leaf tea/infusion, powdered leaf, and standardized extracts in capsules or tablets.
region: Native to tropical and subtropical Asia and widely cultivated or naturalized in other tropical regions.
therapeuticUses: Traditional supportive use; indication depends on species part and preparation.
Evidence level varies; use only within established clinical or traditional context.
traditionalUse: Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine
sideEffects: May cause gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or diarrhea).
Possible headache, dizziness, or allergic skin reaction in sensitive users.
summary: Holy basil (tulsi; usually Ocimum tenuiflorum/O. sanctum) is a traditional medicinal herb with early human trial signals for stress and some metabolic or immune-related outcomes. Current evidence is suggestive but not definitive due to small, heterogeneous studies.
